required.title: [Kit] Basic circuit-building kit: batteries (AA or D-cell), battery holders, insulated copper wire (22-gauge), small light bulbs or LEDs with holders, switches (toggle or knife switches), buzzer, small DC motor

suggested.title: [Kit] Snap Circuits Jr. (SC-100) or Snap Circuits (SC-300)

competence_markers.description: The student can build a complete circuit with a battery, wires, switch, and load (bulb, motor, or buzzer) and explain why it works
The student can explain that electricity flows in a complete loop and demonstrate what happens when the loop is broken
The student understands that a switch controls a circuit by opening and closing a gap in the loop
The student can identify the main electrical panel and main water shutoff valve in their home
The student understands the difference between supply (pressure) and drainage (gravity) water systems
The student can troubleshoot a simple circuit by checking each component systematically
The student understands that household electricity (outlets) is dangerous and not to be touched, while battery circuits at low voltage are safe for learning

content.why_this_matters:
When you flip a light switch, the light comes on. When you turn a faucet, water comes out. When you press a button on the remote, the TV changes channels. These things seem like magic — but they are not magic. They are systems. And every system follows rules.

A system is a group of parts that work together to do something. The electrical system in your house has wires, switches, outlets, and a breaker panel. They all work together to bring electricity from the power company to the lamp on your bedside table. The plumbing system has pipes, valves, and fixtures. They work together to bring clean water in and take dirty water out. Every time you turn on a light or flush a toilet, you are using a system that someone designed, built, and connected.

Right now, most of these systems are invisible to you. The wires are inside the walls. The pipes are under the floor. The ducts that carry warm air from the furnace are hidden in the ceiling. But they are there, and they follow rules that you can learn to understand.

When you understand these rules, something powerful happens: the world stops being mysterious and starts being comprehensible. You know why the light comes on. You know why the water is hot. You know what to check when something stops working. You are not helpless when a system fails — you are the person who can think about it, figure out what went wrong, and maybe fix it.

That is what this packet begins to teach you. Not how to be an electrician or a plumber — but how to understand the systems that make your life work.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Circuit understanding** — the student grasps that electricity flows in a complete loop and a break anywhere stops the flow
2. **Switch and control concepts** — understanding that a switch is a deliberate break that gives you control
3. **Power source awareness** — batteries, outlets, and generators as sources of electricity
4. **Water system basics** — clean water comes in under pressure, dirty water goes out by gravity
5. **Cause-and-effect systems thinking** — seeing the hidden connection between an action (flip a switch) and a result (light turns on)
6. **Safety awareness** — understanding that electricity and water can be dangerous and require respect
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is a Circuit?

Electricity flows through wires the way water flows through pipes. But there is one rule that makes electricity different from almost anything else you have experienced: **electricity only flows in a complete loop.**

This loop is called a circuit. The electricity leaves the battery (or the power source), travels through the wire to the device (a light bulb, a motor, a buzzer), through the device, and back to the battery through another wire. If the loop is complete, the electricity flows and the device works. If the loop is broken anywhere — a disconnected wire, a burned-out bulb, a gap in the path — the electricity stops and nothing works.

This is the single most important idea in all of electrical work: **a complete loop.** Every light switch in your house, every outlet, every appliance — they all depend on a complete loop from the power source, through the device, and back to the power source.

**Build it:** Connect a battery to a light bulb with two wires. The bulb lights up — the circuit is complete. Now disconnect one wire. The bulb goes dark — the circuit is broken. Reconnect it. The bulb lights up again. You have just demonstrated the most fundamental principle of electricity.

### Part 2: Switches — Controlling the Flow

A switch is a deliberate break in a circuit that you control.

When the switch is open (off), there is a gap in the circuit. Electricity cannot flow. The light is off.

When the switch is closed (on), the gap is closed. The circuit is complete. Electricity flows. The light is on.

Every light switch in your house does exactly this. When you flip the switch up, a piece of metal inside the switch closes the gap, completing the circuit. When you flip it down, the metal separates, breaking the circuit. It is that simple.

**Build it:** Add a switch to your battery-and-bulb circuit. Wire it so the switch sits between the battery and the bulb. Open the switch — the bulb is off. Close the switch — the bulb is on. You now have control over the electricity.

**Two switches:** Wire two switches in your circuit, one after the other (in series). Both switches must be closed for the bulb to light. If either switch is open, the circuit is broken and the bulb is dark. This is how safety systems work in many machines — two switches that must both be engaged before the machine operates, so that both of a worker's hands are accounted for and away from the dangerous parts.

### Part 3: Power Sources — Where Electricity Comes From

Electricity does not appear from nowhere. It is generated — created by converting another form of energy into electrical energy.

**Batteries** convert chemical energy into electrical energy. Inside a battery, a chemical reaction pushes electrons through the circuit. When the chemicals are used up, the battery is dead. Batteries provide small amounts of electricity — enough for flashlights, toys, and remote controls, but not enough for a house.

**Power outlets** in your home are connected by wires to a much larger system. A power plant — which might burn fuel, split atoms, or capture wind or sunlight — generates electricity that travels through high-voltage power lines to your neighborhood, through a transformer that reduces the voltage, and through wires to your house. The electricity in your outlets is much more powerful than battery electricity — powerful enough to run a refrigerator, a washing machine, or an air conditioner. And powerful enough to seriously hurt you.

**Generators** convert mechanical energy (something spinning) into electrical energy. A gasoline engine, a water turbine, or a wind turbine spins a generator to produce electricity. Your home may have a backup generator that runs when the power company's electricity is interrupted.

**The safety rule:** Battery circuits at the voltages you are working with (1.5 to 9 volts) are safe to touch and handle. Household outlets (120 volts) are not. Never put anything into an outlet except a proper plug. Never touch a wire or terminal inside an electrical panel. At this age, your work is with battery circuits only. Household electricity is explored in Weathering Storms under careful supervision.

### Part 4: What Electricity Does

Electricity is useful because it can be converted into other forms of energy:

**Light:** Electricity flowing through a light bulb heats a filament (in traditional bulbs) or excites a semiconductor (in LEDs) to produce light. Build a circuit with a bulb or LED to see this conversion.

**Heat:** Electricity flowing through a resistant material produces heat. Your toaster, your electric stove, your space heater, and your hair dryer all convert electricity into heat. (This is also why wires that carry too much electricity get hot — and why that is dangerous.)

**Motion:** Electricity flowing through a motor produces rotation. Build a circuit with a small DC motor and watch the shaft spin. Motors are everywhere — in fans, power tools, washing machines, electric cars, and hundreds of other devices. A motor converts electricity into mechanical work.

**Sound:** Electricity flowing through a speaker or buzzer produces sound. Add a buzzer to your circuit and hear the conversion.

### Part 5: Water Systems — The Other Invisible Infrastructure

Your home has two separate water systems that work together:

**Supply system (water coming in):** Clean water enters your home through a pipe from the water company (or from a well). This water is under pressure — the water company pumps it, or gravity from a water tower pushes it. This pressure is what makes water come out when you turn on a faucet. The water travels through pipes to every sink, toilet, shower, dishwasher, and washing machine in your house.

Somewhere near where the main water pipe enters your home, there is a shutoff valve. If this valve is closed, no water enters the house. Every person in the house should know where this valve is and how to close it — because if a pipe bursts, closing this valve stops the flooding.

**Drainage system (water going out):** Dirty water (from sinks, toilets, showers) leaves your home through a different set of pipes. These pipes are not under pressure — they rely on gravity. The pipes slope downward so water flows out and away from the house to the sewer or septic system.

**The key difference:** Supply pipes are under pressure (water pushes out when you open them). Drain pipes are not under pressure (water flows down by gravity). This is why a broken supply pipe sprays water everywhere (pressure pushes it out) while a broken drain pipe just leaks (gravity pulls it down).

### Part 6: Cause and Effect — Thinking in Systems

Every system you have explored follows the same pattern: something causes something else to happen, and there is a connection between the cause and the effect, even if you cannot see it.

You flip a switch. Inside the wall, a metal contact closes. Electricity flows through a wire to the ceiling. A light bulb converts the electricity to light. The room becomes bright. Five things happened in sequence — but from your perspective, you just flipped a switch and the light came on.

You turn a faucet handle. Inside the faucet, a valve opens. Water under pressure flows through the pipe to the faucet opening. Water comes out. Four things happened — but you just turned a handle and water appeared.

When something stops working, systems thinking asks: **where in the sequence did the break happen?**

The light does not come on. Is the bulb burned out? Is the switch broken? Is the breaker tripped? Is the power out? Each question checks one link in the chain. You start with the simplest and most common cause and work toward the more complex and less common.

This way of thinking — breaking a problem into a chain of causes and effects and checking each link — is the most powerful problem-solving tool you will ever learn. It works for electrical systems. It works for plumbing. It works for engines (2.2.6). It works for understanding why anything in the world does or does not work.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Circuit Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can build a complete circuit from battery to load and back to battery without assistance. They can explain in their own words that electricity needs a complete loop. When a circuit does not work, they check for breaks in the loop as their first step.

**Developing slowly:** The student can build circuits by copying a diagram but cannot explain why the circuit works. They do not make the connection between a break in the circuit and the device not working.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student built enough circuits from scratch (not just following diagrams)? Have they broken and reconnected circuits enough times to internalize the loop concept? Try having them explain the circuit to a younger sibling or stuffed animal — teaching reveals understanding gaps.

### 2. Switch Understanding
**Developing well:** The student understands that a switch opens and closes a gap in the circuit. They can explain why both switches in a series circuit must be closed. They connect this to real switches in their home: "The light switch completes the circuit to the light."

**Developing slowly:** The student uses the switch mechanically but cannot explain what it does electrically. They flip the switch and see the result but have not connected the physical action to the electrical concept.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you opened a wall switch plate (with power off) and shown the student the metal contacts inside? Seeing the physical switch mechanism — the metal pieces that touch and separate — makes the concept concrete.

### 3. Home Systems Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can locate the breaker panel, main water shutoff, and water heater in their home. They can explain in simple terms what each does. They understand that these systems are connected to every light, faucet, and drain in the house.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot remember where systems are located or what they do after the initial tour.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you revisited the home systems more than once? A single tour is not enough. Reference the systems casually in daily life: "The lights just flickered — what controls all the electricity in our house?" "The faucet is dripping — where would we shut off the water if it got worse?"

### 4. Troubleshooting Approach
**Developing well:** When presented with a non-working circuit, the student checks components systematically rather than randomly. They start with the most likely and simplest cause. They can articulate their reasoning: "I am checking the battery first because it is the easiest thing to test."

**Developing slowly:** The student stares at the non-working circuit or randomly disconnects and reconnects wires. They do not have a systematic approach.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled the systematic approach enough times? Have you narrated your own troubleshooting out loud? The student learns the approach by watching you do it, not by being told to do it.
